title: ProcessOwnershipLedger reclaim hooks (session close / failure / staleness / cancel)
goal: Implement reclaim hooks per spec §5.7.5 (AC-PROCESS-LEDGER-RECLAIM). On every session close, failure, staleness detection, and operator cancel (per CX-503D + CX-212G), the reclaim path queries kernel_process_lifecycle for the session's non-stopped rows, calls SandboxAdapter.kill on each, and writes STOP rows with exit_code = -1.
expected_diff_shape: New Rust module src/backend/handshake_core/src/process_ledger/reclaim.rs; integration with SessionBroker (KERNEL-001 primitive) hook points; tests covering all four reclaim trigger modes.
unblock_conditions: MT-007 ledger primitive + write path merged.
implementation_notes: Per spec §5.7.5 reclaim covers four trigger modes: (a) session close (clean), (b) session failure (panic, broker death, abnormal exit), (c) staleness (no heartbeat within configured TTL — default 5min), (d) operator cancel (explicit kill). The reclaim primitive: `Reclaim::run(session_id: SessionId, trigger: ReclaimTrigger) -> ReclaimReport` where ReclaimTrigger is the enum {Close, Failure, Stale, OperatorCancel}. Internally: (1) SELECT process_uuid, sandbox_adapter_id, engine_kind FROM kernel_process_lifecycle WHERE parent_session_id = $1 AND stopped_at IS NULL FOR UPDATE; (2) for each row, locate the SandboxAdapter via a registry (the SandboxAdapter trait lives in cluster B; this MT defines a placeholder trait surface `trait SandboxKill { fn kill(&self, process_uuid: Uuid) -> Result<(), KillError>; }` with a TODO marker referencing cluster B for the real adapter binding); (3) call kill(); (4) write STOP row with exit_code = -1, stopped_at = now(), via the perf-safe writer from MT-007. ReclaimReport carries `{ session_id, trigger, processes_reclaimed: Vec<{process_uuid, kill_result}>, total_duration_ms }`. SessionBroker integration: this MT exposes a `reclaim_handle()` that SessionBroker can call from its session-close path (note: SessionBroker exists from KERNEL-001; if its actual API does not yet have a reclaim hook, add one in the cluster X integration pass — this MT just exposes the entry point). Staleness detection: a background tokio task scans for sessions whose last heartbeat is older than TTL every 30s; on hit, calls Reclaim::run(session_id, ReclaimTrigger::Stale). Tests use a mock SandboxKill impl: (a) clean close kills 0 hanging processes when session exited cleanly; (b) failure kills N pending processes; (c) stale detection fires after TTL; (d) operator cancel kills immediately. Per HBR-QUIET-003 evidence_kind=process_lifecycle_test_run this is exactly the test family this MT must produce.
risk_if_missed: Without reclaim, orphan processes accumulate from crashed sessions; violates HBR-QUIET-003 'no orphan processes after a run, failure, or kill'.
validator_focus: All four ReclaimTrigger variants have explicit tests; STOP row is always written even when kill() fails (kill_result captures the error); staleness detection runs as a background task without blocking the main event loop.

builder_handoff_correction_20260525.current_interpretation: Kernel Builder implementation handoff readiness only; no WP/Integration Validator pass or validator-reviewed completion is claimed.

lifecycle.ready_for_validation_reason: Kernel Builder believes implementation/supporting evidence is ready for Integration Validator handoff; validator review remains pending.

minimum_controls: STOP row is written even when kill() returns an error (so post-hoc audit knows the process was attempted to be reclaimed).
Staleness TTL is configurable and the default is documented in implementation notes.
FOR UPDATE row-lock prevents two reclaim runs racing on the same session.

integration_validator_review.verdict_reason: Reclaim correctly queries kernel_process_lifecycle with FOR UPDATE row-lock (preventing two reclaim runs racing per the red-team control), writes a STOP row regardless of kill outcome (satisfies the audit-trail control), and tracks KillOutcome::Failed with the error message. However the reclaim loop runs sequentially over active processes: for a session with N processes, each sandbox.kill() is awaited serially — a slow/hung kill blocks the rest. No timeout, no parallel reclaim.
hbr_unsatisfied: HBR-SWARM-002 partially — leases-and-cancellation behavior is correct per-process but multi-process reclaim has no cancellation/timeout

However the reclaim loop runs sequentially over active processes: for a session with N processes, each sandbox.kill() is awaited serially — a slow/hung kill blocks the rest. No timeout, no parallel reclaim.
